title: FastText vs. Word2Vec
concept: FastText is far better at handling morphologically rich languages and spelling errors, but requires more memory to store subword tables.

exercises.prompt: Which model breaks words down into smaller subword chunks?
exercises.explain: Word2Vec learns patterns based on whole words, while FastText is designed to look inside words at their smaller subword parts.

cards.title: Handling Typos and New Words
cards.body: Imagine typing 'aple' instead of 'apple'. A basic AI might get totally confused. How do modern search engines and translation tools still understand what you mean, even with typos? Let's find out!

cards.title: What's the Difference?
cards.body: While Word2Vec treats every word as a single unbroken unit, FastText breaks words down into smaller character chunks called subwords. This allows FastText to understand typos, root words, and brand new words it has never seen before. However, storing all these tiny chunks requires much more computer memory!

cards.title: The Building Block Analogy
cards.body: Think of Word2Vec like a dictionary of whole words. If you lookup 'unhappy' and it's not there, you get nothing. FastText is like a dictionary of building blocks (prefixes, roots, suffixes). Even if it has never seen 'unhappy', it knows 'un-' means 'not' and 'happy' means 'glad', so it can figure out the meaning.

cards.title: FastText in Action
cards.body: If you train Word2Vec on the word 'creating', it learns nothing about 'creator' or 'creates'. If you train FastText, it breaks 'creating' into chunks like 'cre', 'rea', 'eat'. When it sees 'creator', it recognizes the shared chunks and instantly knows they are related!

cards.title: Is FastText Always Better?
cards.body: Many believe that because FastText is newer and smarter with spelling errors, it is always the best choice. In reality, FastText needs a massive amount of memory (RAM) to store its giant table of subword chunks. For simple systems with limited memory, Word2Vec is still a lightweight champion!

cards.title: Under the Hood
cards.body: Compare how the two models process the word 'learning' under the hood.

left.text: Treats 'learning' as one single block. If it sees 'learns', it treats it as a completely different block.

right.text: Breaks 'learning' into 'lea', 'ear', 'arn', 'rni', 'nin', 'ing'. It shares these pieces with similar words.

exercises.prompt: What is the main structural difference between Word2Vec and FastText?

options.text: FastText only works with numbers, while Word2Vec works with text.
FastText breaks words down into subword chunks, while Word2Vec only looks at whole words.
Word2Vec requires much more memory to run than FastText.
Word2Vec is better at handling spelling errors.

exercises.explain: Word2Vec treats words as single, indivisible units, whereas FastText breaks words down into smaller subword chunks (like 'sub', 'word', etc.).

exercises.prompt: True or False: FastText is better than Word2Vec at understanding misspelled words.
exercises.explain: Because FastText breaks words into smaller chunks, it can match chunks of a misspelled word (like 'receeve') to the correct word ('receive'). Word2Vec cannot do this.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe a downside of FastText:
exercises.sentence: FastText requires more _ than Word2Vec because it stores subword pieces.
exercises.explain: FastText breaks words down into many small pieces (subwords). Storing all these subword pieces takes up a lot more storage space (memory) compared to Word2Vec.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following statements about FastText and Word2Vec is true?

options.text: FastText is better at handling spelling mistakes and typos.
Word2Vec breaks words into tiny character pieces to handle typos.
Word2Vec is better at handling languages with complex word endings.

exercises.explain: Option B is incorrect because Word2Vec treats words as whole units and cannot break them down. Option C is incorrect because FastText is actually better for languages with complex word endings. Option A is correct.

exercises.prompt: You deploy an AI chatbot using Word2Vec, and a user types a brand new slang word 'un-happy-ish' that wasn't in the training data. What happens?

options.text: Word2Vec will accurately guess the word by looking at similar-sounding words.
Word2Vec will automatically retrain its entire neural network to learn the word.
Word2Vec will fail because it cannot handle Out-Of-Vocabulary (OOV) words.
Word2Vec will use subword pieces like 'un-' and '-happy' to figure it out.

exercises.explain: Word2Vec fails completely when meeting new words because it only knows exact whole words from its training. FastText uses subwords (character n-grams) to break down and understand unseen words or spelling mistakes.
